channel.title: Dhaka Tribune - Opinion
channel.description: Op-Eds, Editorials and Longform from Dhaka Tribune

item.title: Getting our hospitals operational
item.description:
The directive from the prime minister to immediately operationalize six long-unused children’s hospitals across Bangladesh is undoubtedly a promising development, and one that could bring meaningful relief to a healthcare system that has been strained by overcrowding, underfunding, and unequal access to specialized treatment for too long.
That these facilities, reportedly built at a cost of Tk320 crore, have remained idle for years is itself deeply troubling. However, the government’s latest intervention at least signals recognition that such waste can no longer be tolerated.
Bangladesh’s public healthcare infrastructure continues to suffer from a severe imbalance between urban and regional access. For countless families outside Dhaka, obtaining advanced pediatric care often means expensive travel, overcrowded hospitals, and delayed treatment.
If hospitals in Rangpur, Khulna, Rajshahi, Barishal, Sylhet, and Comilla are finally made functional, they could significantly reduce pressure on major urban medical centres while improving healthcare access for millions of people in underserved regions.
The proposed Bangladesh-China Friendship Hospital in Nilphamari also carries major potential: Northern Bangladesh has long lagged behind in terms of advanced medical facilities, despite serving a massive population.
A regional referral centre there could, therefore, improve treatment availability for more complex illnesses while reducing the financial and logistical burden faced by patients forced to seek care elsewhere.
Still, Bangladesh has seen one too many ambitious healthcare announcements lose momentum amid bureaucratic delays, staffing shortages, and poor coordination.
Buildings alone do not create healthcare systems; doctors, nurses, equipment, maintenance, and accountability are equally essential.
The government’s insistence on progress reports and rapid implementation is undoubtedly encouraging, but execution will ultimately determine whether these projects become transformative public assets or merely more expensive administrative failures.
For now, this initiative deserves cautious optimism -- provided the urgency shown today is sustained tomorrow.

item.title: A police force for the people
item.description:
With Police Week 2026 commencing, this is an important opportunity not merely for ceremonial activities, but for serious reflection on the state of law enforcement in Bangladesh.
The police remain one of the most visible institutions of the state, carrying the immense responsibility of maintaining public order, combating crime, and ensuring citizen safety in an increasingly complex social environment. Their contribution, particularly under difficult circumstances and resource constraints, certainly deserves recognition.
Yet any meaningful discussion about policing must also confront the challenges that continue to undermine public confidence.
For many ordinary citizens, interactions with law enforcement are too often associated with delays, harassment, political influence, or an inconsistent application of the law.
This perception, whether fully justified or not, has invariably weakened the relationship between the police and the people they are meant to serve.
Professionalism and accountability must therefore remain at the centre of reform efforts. Modern policing cannot rely solely on force or authority; it must be rooted in public trust.
This requires stronger oversight mechanisms, better training, improved working conditions, and a firm commitment to impartiality, regardless of political or social status.
Officers who abuse power must face consequences, just as officers who perform their duties with integrity should be rewarded and protected.
There is also an urgent need to invest in community-oriented policing.
Citizens are far more likely to cooperate with law enforcement when they view officers as accessible and fair rather than distant and intimidating.
Technology and modernization initiatives can assist policing efforts but, ultimately, institutional culture matters more than equipment alone.
This Police Week should therefore serve not only as a celebration of service, but as a reminder that effective policing depends on mutual trust between law enforcement and the public.
Without that trust, no amount of modernization can fully ensure public safety or justice.

item.title: Measles and the classroom
item.description:
Bangladesh has recently witnessed a rise in measles cases in several parts of the country, drawing attention once again to the importance of child health, immunisation awareness, and community cooperation.
Health authorities, alongside international organizations, continue to work actively to strengthen vaccination efforts and raise public awareness regarding disease prevention.
While measles is primarily considered a public health issue, it is also closely connected to education.
Schools are among the most important spaces in a child’s life, and educational institutions can play a meaningful role in promoting awareness, encouraging healthy practices, and supporting families during times of public health concern.
According to the World Health Organization, measles remains one of the most contagious viral diseases in the world, particularly affecting children who are unvaccinated or only partially vaccinated.
Common symptoms include fever, cough, runny nose, and skin rash, while severe complications may arise in vulnerable children if not addressed early. The WHO continues to emphasize the importance of maintaining strong immunisation coverage to protect communities.
Bangladesh has long been recognized internationally for its progress in childhood immunisation and public health initiatives. Recent nationwide efforts by the government demonstrate continued commitment towards safeguarding children through vaccination campaigns and awareness programs.
At the same time, the current situation offers an important opportunity for educators and school leaders to reflect on how schools can contribute positively to student well-being beyond academics.
Schools as centres of awareness
Schools are uniquely positioned to support public awareness because they bring together children, parents, teachers, and communities every day.
Educational institutions can help reinforce simple but important health practices such as:
For younger children especially, schools often shape habits that last a lifetime.
Age-appropriate classroom discussions on health and wellbeing can also help children develop responsibility towards themselves and others.
When children learn the importance of care, cleanliness, empathy, and community safety, they become active participants in creating healthier environments.
The importance of parent-school collaboration
Strong parent-school partnerships become even more valuable during periods of increased health concern.
Schools can support families by:
In many cases, parents look to schools not only for education, but also for guidance and reassurance. Compassionate communication can therefore make a meaningful difference.
Attendance and student well-being
Educational leaders are increasingly recognising the importance of monitoring student well-being alongside academic progress.
Sudden changes in attendance patterns, fatigue, or signs of illness may indicate the need for additional support and communication with families.
This does not mean schools replace healthcare systems. Rather, it highlights how schools and health services can complement one another in supporting children holistically.
The modern classroom is no longer focused solely on textbooks and examinations. Today’s schools are also expected to nurture emotional wellbeing, safety, social responsibility, and healthy habits.
Moving forward together
Bangladesh has consistently shown resilience and community spirit during times of challenge.
The collaborative efforts currently being undertaken by health authorities, development partners, educators, and families reflect a shared commitment towards protecting children and strengthening public awareness.
As educators, we are reminded that schools hold an important place within society. Beyond academic instruction, schools help shape informed, responsible, and caring future citizens.
In moments like these, the partnership between health and education becomes especially important.
Protecting children is a shared responsibility, and when families, schools, communities, and public institutions work together, meaningful progress becomes possible.
Maryam Noor Yunus is Principal, New Horizon Crescent International School.

item.title: The need for accountability in addressing the measles crisis
item.description: The measles situation in Bangladesh reveals the shambolic state of child health management....
